name: Garcia Exploration 45

facts.value: CE Category A (Open Ocean), per the owner's manual — certified for extended voyages where wind may exceed Force 8 and waves may exceed 4 metres. This is the highest CE design category and confirms the boat is built for true offshore use.

facts.value: Reported issues: (1) Infant mortality in mechanical gearboxes with standard Volvo Penta D2-75 engines; (2) Leaking hatches and instrument seals on early hulls (Version 1); (3) Aluminum hull + fiberglass deck joint negates unitary aluminum advantage—dissimilar material joint prone to chronic leaks over time; (4) Early cockpit visibility obstructed by high coachroof (fixed in later versions with hard dodger windows)

sources.title: Blue Water Sailing — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
Cruisers Forum — Garcia Exploration 45 Discussion
YBW Forum — Garcia Yachts Discussion
Practical Boat Owner — Garcia Exploration 45 Review

facts.value: Garcia yachts built in Cherbourg, France; parts availability through Garcia service centers and authorized dealers. Standard systems (Volvo Penta engines, Webasto heaters, B&G electronics) widely available. Aluminum hull components may require factory or specialized fabrication.

sources.title: Garcia Yachts Official

facts.value: Hull and deck by Olivier Racoupeau (Racoupeau Yacht Design), interior by Garcia. Built by Garcia Yachts in Cherbourg, France, part of the Grand Large Yachting group (alongside Allures and Outremer) since 2010. Output is roughly ten boats per year; the Exploration 45 and 52 are the group's best-selling expedition monohulls, with build lead times reported around 18 months.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat
no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg

facts.value: Welded marine-grade aluminium hull (5083 H111 plate with 6060 T6 extruded profiles on at least one documented boat), built stress-free over a welded frame. Garcia builds an all-aluminium hull AND deck (a true metal monocoque), which distinguishes it from sister-brand Allures whose deck is GRP. This means no osmosis, exceptional impact resistance, and field-repairability: in Garcia's words aluminium 'might bend, but it doesn't break — and it's easily repaired, even far from any marina.'

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts
no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg
Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats

facts.value: Garcia's expedition range uses thick bottom plating for grounding and ice contact. The larger Exploration 52 is documented at 6mm above the waterline, 8mm below, and 10mm on the floor — heavy enough to push through thin ice and to dry out on a flat bottom. The 45 follows the same construction philosophy; an exact published plate schedule for the 45 was not located. The near-plumb bow carries an icebreaker/towing eye at the foot of the stem.

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 52 test — Yachting World
Garcia Exploration 52 — yacht.de test

facts.value: Integral aluminium centreboard that retracts fully into the hull (not a ballasted keel). Draft is about 1.05m board up and 2.8m board down. Designer Jimmy Cornell reports the boat points as close as 35 degrees with the board down. The board is raised by a single rope-and-winch tackle. On Cornell's own boat the board did its job during a hard grounding in the Northwest Passage — it 'swung up, scraped along the top of the rock, then dropped back into its lowered position.'

sources.title: Cornell Sailing — Aventura IV / Exploration 45 FAQ
Jimmy Cornell: Why aluminium centreboarders are the best choice — Yachting World

facts.value: Twin aluminium rudders, each protected by a short, robust skeg and running on JEFA self-aligning bearings. The rudder shafts are deliberately oversized so the blades can take the loads of the stern when the boat is dried out on its flat bottom. A central skeg also houses and protects the propeller and shaft. With the centreboard up there is no keel to act as a pivot, which Cornell notes makes steering in strong following winds easy and 'virtually eliminates' the risk of broaching.

sources.title: Practical Boat Owner — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg
Jimmy Cornell: Why aluminium centreboarders are the best choice — Yachting World

facts.value: Tankage figures vary slightly by source and market. The Yachting Monthly review lists 500 litres water and 700 litres fuel; the US 'Arctic Monkey' brokerage listing lists 132 US gallons (about 500 litres) water and 87 US gallons (about 330 litres) fuel plus 29 gallons holding. Tanks sit low in the bilge, keeping weight central and low. Either way this is generous water for an expedition 45-footer, and a watermaker is normally fitted to extend autonomy.

sources.title: Yachting Monthly — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts

facts.value: A heavy-displacement expedition boat (roughly 14 tonnes / 31,000 lb) that is comfortable and easily driven off the wind but pays the usual centreboarder penalty to windward because there is little weight low down. SAIL recorded it 'fully powered up at about a 40-degree apparent-wind angle, making better than 6 knots in a 20-knot breeze in choppy conditions,' with a light helm carrying just a touch of weather helm. Reviewers consistently describe the motion in a seaway as remarkably smooth for a monohull. It is slow in light air and a bow thruster is a near-essential option for marina handling.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat
Yachting Monthly — Garcia Exploration 45 Review

facts.value: Jimmy Cornell's own Exploration 45, Aventura IV, has a genuine high-latitude track record: two crossings of the Drake Passage to Antarctica, a transit of the Northwest Passage, and cruising in Alaska, including a hard grounding the centreboard absorbed and a collision with an underwater ledge of an iceberg. On a Greenland-to-England passage with engine trouble, the boat's solar, wind (D400) and Sail-Gen hydro charging covered all electrical needs unaided. This is sustained expedition use, not a single delivery anecdote.

sources.title: Cornell Sailing — Aventura IV / Exploration 45 FAQ
Jimmy Cornell: Why aluminium centreboarders are the best choice — Yachting World

facts.value: Real-world durability is strong. A sister Garcia Exploration 52 hauled out after roughly 50,000 nautical miles and a circumnavigation needed essentially cosmetic attention only — some antifouling peeled and minor dents, 'but nothing more.' The sacrificial anodes had done their job and worn down as intended, with no galvanic damage to the hull and no major equipment failures recorded at that survey. This supports Garcia's claim that a properly protected aluminium hull 'ages very well.'

sources.title: no-frills-sailing — Garcia Exploration 52 after sailing around the world (the big D-Check)
Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats

facts.value: An aluminium hull demands specific, disciplined care rather than constant work: keep correctly specified sacrificial anodes (zinc or aluminium alloy — never copper-based, and avoid mixing anode types), use only antifouling formulated for aluminium (no copper-based paints in contact with the hull), fit a galvanic isolator or isolation transformer on shore power, and stay vigilant for stray current in marinas, which is the main external corrosion driver. Garcia keeps the electrical system electrically isolated from the hull by design so that galvanic corrosion 'can only be induced from the outside.' Inspect hull, anodes and fittings frequently to catch problems early.

sources.title: Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats
More Thoughts on Galvanic Corrosion for Aluminium Yachts — Katamarans

facts.value: A niche, expensive boat that holds value well. Current new builds list around €830,000-€1,000,000 (roughly $830k-$1.04M) ex-yard depending on specification. On the used market, 2021-2025 examples have been listed roughly $725,000-$870,000; an early, very-well-equipped 2015 boat (Arctic Monkey) was offered at $549,000. Production is limited (about ten boats a year), so supply of used examples is thin and asking prices stay firm.

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 45 boats for sale — YachtWorld
Garcia Exploration 45 boats for sale — Boatshop24
Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts

facts.value: Won SAIL Magazine's Best Boat / Boat of the Year award for 2015 and was named European Yacht of the Year in the Luxury Cruiser category — unusually strong recognition for a purpose-built expedition aluminium centreboarder rather than a mainstream production cruiser.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat

praise: Named 'Best Boat' and 'Boat of the Year' by Sail Magazine in 2015
Awarded European Yacht of the Year in Luxury Cruiser category
Owner Arctic Monkey praised for excellent handling in 30+ knot Atlantic gales while maintaining 10-13 knot speeds
Owners describe boat as 'absolutely bulletproof' for serious cruising and offshore passages
270° pilothouse visibility from forward nav station highly praised for confidence during rough weather and offshore watch-keeping
Twin rudders with sacrificial top sections provide excellent helm feel, better than competing designs
Interior comfort from polar cold to tropical heat consistently praised; watertight doors create quiet, comfortable refuge in gales
Exceptional storage capacity (~2 tons) for long-distance cruising provisioning and spares
Aluminum hull provides superior impact resistance (3-12mm thickness)
Centerboard allows access to shallow anchorages unreachable by deep-keel yachts while maintaining stability
Easy single-handed or reduced-crew handling with all lines led to cockpit
Excellent ventilation and heating systems appropriate for all climates
complaints: Infant mortality gearbox failures reported on mechanical boxes paired with standard Volvo Penta D2-75 engines
Early production hulls experienced water ingress through deck hatches and instrument seals (leaking hatch problem over years)
Aluminum hull + fiberglass deck joint represents design weakness: chronic leaks expected over time due to dissimilar material expansion/contraction
Cockpit visibility obstructed by high coachroof in Version 1 (corrected with hard dodger windows in later versions)
Heavy and not exciting boat to sail; slow in light winds due to high displacement
Centerboard configuration reduces windward performance compared to deep keel designs (gains downwind when raised)
Tight quarters when maneuvering in marinas (large 14-ton boat, needs bow thruster consideration)
Multiple interior access steps considered awkward by some (though designers argue aids safety in rough seas)
known_issues: Volvo Penta D2-75 gearbox early failure—clutch failures repeat within 3 years; Volvo extended warranty but limited labor coverage
Leaking hatches and instrument seals on early Version 1 hulls requiring pressure testing
Aluminum-to-fiberglass deck joint structural vulnerability — builders chose fiberglass for cost/complexity reduction but sacrificed unitary metal construction benefit
Initial cockpit hard-dodger visibility issues corrected in later production runs

sources.title: VDV Marine — Understanding Marine Condition Survey Costs
Trawler Forum — Survey Costs Discussion

refit_items.action: Re-bedding/replacement of leaking deck hatches and instrument seals; pressure testing of technical room and lockers

sources.title: Practical Boat Owner — Garcia Exploration 45 Review

refit_items.action: Sacrificial anode replacement; electrical grounding system inspection and repair for galvanic corrosion prevention

sources.title: Garcia Yachts Official Specifications

refit_items.action: Soda-blast or peel old antifouling, inspect bare hull for pitting, apply an aluminium-safe barrier/primer scheme and copper-free antifouling. The wrong (copper-based) paint in contact with the hull is actively harmful, so this is not interchangeable with a routine GRP repaint.

sources.title: Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats
More Thoughts on Galvanic Corrosion for Aluminium Yachts — Katamarans

refit_items.action: Fit or upgrade a galvanic isolator or isolation transformer and verify the hull stays isolated from the DC negative/bonding system. Cheap insurance against the marina stray current that is the main external corrosion threat to an aluminium hull.

sources.title: More Thoughts on Galvanic Corrosion for Aluminium Yachts — Katamarans
Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats

refit_items.action: Bring an older boat to expedition standard: service or replace the Webasto hydronic heater, add/upgrade lithium house bank with Watt & Sea hydrogenerator and solar, fit redundant autopilot drive and offshore comms (Starlink/Iridium). The boat is designed for this; budget reflects bringing a 2015-era fit-out current.

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts
Cornell Sailing — Aventura IV / Exploration 45 FAQ

refit_items.action: Drop the board, inspect pivot pin and trunk for wear and debris, replace the lifting rope/cable and winch service kit, check anode and antifoul inside the case. A simple rope-and-winch system but neglected examples seize; service at haul-out.

sources.title: Garcia Yachting Exploration 45 Owner's Manual — ManualsLib

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo Penta D2-55/D2-75 Engine Spares Kit (Oil filters, fuel filters, impellers, gasket set, fuel transfer pump)
provisioning_specific.why: Gearbox infant mortality and repeat repairs documented; engine is production standard; critical for autonomous operation in remote areas

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto Thermo 90ST Hydronic Heater Service Kit (Glow plugs, gaskets, burner chamber, cleaning kit)
provisioning_specific.why: Standard heater across fleet; essential for high-latitude cruising where heating failure is mission-critical; service kits prolong intervals

provisioning_specific.item: Centerboard Cable & Winch Mechanism Spares (Rope, pulleys, bearing grease, gasket seals)
provisioning_specific.why: Unique to centerboard design; shallow-draft access is core advantage; loss of centerboard operation limits tactical options in difficult weather

provisioning_specific.item: Aluminum Hull Sacrificial Anodes (Zinc or aluminum, sized for hull+keel profile)
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum hull requires diligent galvanic protection; pitting corrosion in high-saline conditions can accelerate; anodes must be inspected/replaced annually in heavy cruising

provisioning_specific.item: Deck Hatch & Instrument Seal Compound (Marine-grade silicone, polysulfide sealant, butyl tape)
provisioning_specific.why: Early production hulls had chronic hatch leaks; aluminum-to-fiberglass joint vulnerability noted; seal maintenance prevents water ingress into technical room and stowage

provisioning_specific.item: Hard Dodger & Saloon Window Gasket Kit (Clear acrylic replacement panes, stainless hardware)
provisioning_specific.why: 270° pilothouse vision critical for safe offshore watch-keeping; Version 1 boats had visibility issues; replacement panes and seals essential for multi-year cruise

provisioning_specific.item: Dessalator Watermaker Spares (Filters, membrane cartridge, solenoid valve, high-pressure pump seals)
provisioning_specific.why: Optional but standard on many cruising builds; failure leaves crew dependent on water reserves; membrane replacement every 2-3 years in heavy use

provisioning_specific.item: B&G Autopilot Spares (NAC3 processor fuses, Triton controller circuit boards, wiring harness, ram seals)
provisioning_specific.why: Production standard autopilot; redundant design documented; critical for offshore passage-making and fatigue management in long passages

provisioning_specific.item: Victron Battery & Electrical System Spares (Fuses, breakers, alternator brushes, battery terminal lugs, isolation transformer cooling fan)
provisioning_specific.why: Substantial battery bank (1,000 ah potential) and redundant charging (dual alternators + solar + hydro); electrical failure compromises refrigeration, heating, navigation, and propulsion

provisioning_specific.item: Twin Rudder & Skeg Replacement Hardware (Rudder stock seals, bearing assemblies, pintles, gudgeons, skeg bolts)
provisioning_specific.why: Twin rudders with sacrificial top sections are differentiator; bearing wear and seal leakage common in high-load offshore use; spare hardware enables at-sea repair

provisioning_specific.item: Aluminium-safe antifouling and barrier-coat stock plus matching topcoat for touch-ups
provisioning_specific.why: Copper-based antifouling in contact with the hull is actively harmful. Carry the correct aluminium-compatible paint system so the bottom can be touched up or recoated anywhere without grabbing whatever the local yard stocks.

provisioning_specific.item: Galley provisioning fit-out: dual fridge drawers plus dedicated freezer spares (compressor, gasket, thermostat), vacuum sealer and bulk dry-stores in the ~2 ton stowage
provisioning_specific.why: The boat offers two front-loading fridge drawers under the galley and a separate freezer, with roughly two tonnes of stowage for an expedition payload. For high-latitude or remote passages, carry compressor and gasket spares so a single fridge failure does not spoil weeks of provisions.

provisioning_specific.item: Watermaker membrane, pre-filters and high-pressure pump seal kit (Dessalator/Echotec depending on build)
provisioning_specific.why: Around 500 litres of tankage is generous but a watermaker is the real autonomy enabler on a long expedition. Membranes and seal kits are not stocked in remote cruising grounds; carry a 12-month consumable supply plus one spare membrane.

gaps: Angle of Vanishing Stability (AVS) — Not published anywhere we could find. This is the single most useful self-righting number, and its absence is the main reason the stability score is not higher. Centreboard boats of this type are sometimes described as having lower ultimate stability than deep-keel designs, but no specific AVS figure exists to confirm or deny that.
STIX — The owner's manual quotes a full-load value of 37.5, but it comes from a single source and we could not cross-check it.

Hull plate thickness for the 45 specifically — Not published; we have the 52's schedule (6mm above waterline, 8mm below, 10mm floor) as a guide to the build philosophy, but not the 45's exact figures.

Survival-storm behaviour above Force 9 — The high-latitude and 30-40 knot track record is well documented, but we did not find a detailed first-hand account of heaving-to, lying to a drogue, or genuine survival conditions.
Standing rigging interval for the cutter rig — No Garcia-specific replacement age or interval is published; general marine practice was used.

Parts lead times and aluminium-repair availability outside Europe — Garcia supports the boats from Cherbourg, but real-world lead times for hull/coachroof composite repairs and spares in remote cruising grounds are not documented.

Tropical airflow performance in very hot anchorages — The ventilation hardware (Dorade boxes plus forced ventilation) is confirmed, but measured comfort in extreme heat (Red Sea, SE Asia) is not documented.

sources.quote: Wind speeds reached 30+ knots, with gusts hitting 40 knots; The boat surfed at 10-13 knots consistently; Maximum recorded speed was 15.5 knots

cleaned_facts.value: General 45-ft boat standing rigging replacement in $5,000–$10,000 range; no Garcia-specific interval documented

sources.title: Improve Sailing — Average Cost to Replace Standing Rigging
sources.quote: A 40-foot sailboat may require replacement...which could cost around $6,000 to $8,000; medium-sized category (30 to 50 feet): $5,000 to $10,000

cleaned_facts.value: Confirmed: (1) Volvo D2-75 gearbox clutch failures repeating every ~200 hours, widespread production defect; (2) GRP cabin top on aluminum hull creates dissimilar material joint prone to leaks and welding quality concerns; (3) Early cockpit visibility obstructed by high coachroof; (4) Heavy displacement makes boat slow in light winds

sources.title: YBW Forum — Garcia Yachts Discussion
sources.quote: Some of the welding around the bow area was dubious looking (full of pits and crevices!); fiberglass deck/cabin tops glued and bolted to aluminum structure

sources.title: YachtForums — Volvo D2-75 Gearbox Problems
sources.quote: The failure of the gear system...repeating each 200 hours approximately; five gearbox repairs since 2007

cleaned_facts.value: Garcia built in Cherbourg, France; standard systems (Volvo Penta, Webasto, Victron, B&G) widely available globally; aluminum hull repairs require specialized fabrication

sources.title: Garcia Yachts Official

cleaned_facts.value: Hull and deck designed by Olivier Racoupeau (Racoupeau Yacht Design); built by Garcia Yachts, Cherbourg, in the Grand Large Yachting group (with Allures and Outremer) since 2010. Roughly ten boats a year; the 45 and 52 are the group's best-selling expedition monohulls.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat
sources.quote: designer is Racoupeau Yacht Design; builder is Garcia Yachting (France)

sources.title: no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg
sources.quote: Garcia maintains full aluminum monocoques throughout; the Exploration 45 and the 52 are the most sold

cleaned_facts.value: Welded marine-grade aluminium hull AND deck (true metal monocoque), 5083 H111 plate with 6060 T6 profiles documented on at least one boat. Distinguished from sister-brand Allures, which uses a GRP deck. No osmosis; high impact resistance; field-repairable.

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts
sources.quote: Aluminum 5083 H111 with 6060 T6 extruded profiles

sources.title: no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg
sources.quote: Garcia yachts use all-aluminum construction... Allures... The deck is made of GRP

sources.title: Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats
sources.quote: It might bend, but it doesn't break — and it's easily repaired, even far from any marina

cleaned_facts.value: Exact plate schedule for the 45 not published in available sources. The larger Exploration 52, built to the same philosophy, is documented at 6mm above waterline, 8mm below, 10mm floor — thick enough to push through thin ice and dry out. Near-plumb bow with icebreaker/towing eye at the stem foot.

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 52 — yacht.de test
sources.quote: aluminium plates of six (above the waterline) and eight millimetres (below)... plates ten millimetres thick for the floor element

cleaned_facts.value: Integral aluminium centreboard retracting fully into the hull; draft ~1.05m up / 2.8m down; points to ~35 degrees board down per Cornell; single rope-and-winch lift. Board absorbed a hard Northwest Passage grounding by swinging up.

sources.title: Cornell Sailing — Aventura IV / Exploration 45 FAQ
sources.quote: an integral centreboard... when the board is raised, it is fully retracted into the hull... a draft of 2.8 metres... sail as close as 35 degrees to the wind

sources.title: Jimmy Cornell: Why aluminium centreboarders are the best choice — Yachting World
sources.quote: the centreboard did its job and swung up, scraped along the top of the rock, then dropped back into its lowered position

cleaned_facts.value: Twin aluminium rudders on short protective skegs, JEFA self-aligning bearings, oversized shafts to take drying loads; central skeg houses and protects prop and shaft. Board up, no keel to pivot on — Cornell notes easy downwind steering and 'virtually eliminated' broaching.

sources.title: Practical Boat Owner — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
sources.quote: Twin aluminium rudders protected against collision by robust, short skegs... JEFA self-aligning bearings

sources.title: no-frills-sailing — At Allures and Garcia Yachts of Cherbourg
sources.quote: Oversized aluminum rudder shafts designed to handle loads of the boat's stern part when falling dry

cleaned_facts.value: Water about 500 litres; fuel figures differ by source (Yachting Monthly 700 litres; US Arctic Monkey listing 87 US gal / ~330 litres). Tanks low in the bilge for central low weight. Generous water for the size; watermaker normally fitted.

sources.title: Yachting Monthly — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
sources.quote: Fuel: 700 litres (154 gal); Water: 500 litres (110 gal)

sources.title: Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts
sources.quote: Fuel: 87 gallons; Fresh water: 132 gallons; Holding: 29 gallons

cleaned_facts.value: Heavy expedition boat (~14 t / 31,000 lb); comfortable and easily driven off the wind, weaker to windward (centreboarder, little low weight). SAIL: powered up at ~40 degrees AWA, better than 6 kn in 20 kn; light helm; smooth seaway motion; slow in light air; bow thruster near-essential.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat
sources.quote: fully powered up at about a 40-degree apparent-wind angle, making better than 6 knots in a 20-knot breeze in choppy conditions

sources.title: Yachting Monthly — Garcia Exploration 45 Review
sources.quote: windward performance suffers... lack of weight low down... a bow thruster is an option I wouldn't be without

cleaned_facts.value: Jimmy Cornell's own Aventura IV (Exploration 45) has crossed the Drake Passage to Antarctica twice, transited the Northwest Passage and cruised Alaska, survived a hard grounding (board swung up) and an iceberg-ledge collision, and on a Greenland-England passage with engine trouble met all power needs from solar, D400 wind and Sail-Gen hydro alone.

sources.title: Cornell Sailing — Aventura IV / Exploration 45 FAQ
sources.quote: twice across the Drake Passage to Antarctica... the Northwest Passage... Alaska... colliding with an iceberg's underwater ledge... D400 wind generator, Sail-Gen hydro-generator, and a 140 watts solar panel... have fulfilled our energy needs on all offshore passages

cleaned_facts.value: A sister Exploration 52 hauled out after ~50,000 nm and a circumnavigation needed essentially cosmetic work only — some antifouling peeled, minor dents, 'nothing more.' Anodes had worn as intended with no galvanic hull damage and no major failures at survey.

sources.title: no-frills-sailing — Garcia Exploration 52 after sailing around the world (the big D-Check)
sources.quote: just some of the antifouling has been peeled off, some dents are visible but nothing more... those [anodes] had been suffering and making their sacrifice for the boat pretty well

cleaned_facts.value: Aluminium needs specific care: correctly specified zinc/aluminium anodes (never copper-based, do not mix types), antifouling formulated for aluminium only, galvanic isolator or isolation transformer on shore power, and vigilance for marina stray current. Garcia isolates the electrical system from the hull so corrosion 'can only be induced from the outside.' Inspect hull/anodes/fittings frequently.

sources.title: Garcia Yachts — Aluminium blue water cruising sailboats
sources.quote: Properly protected by sacrificial anodes, an aluminium hull ages very well... galvanic corrosion can only be induced from the outside

sources.title: More Thoughts on Galvanic Corrosion for Aluminium Yachts — Katamarans
sources.quote: Power supplies in marinas often leak small amounts of stray current... galvanic isolators or cathodic protection systems can help mitigate stray current issues

cleaned_facts.value: New builds list roughly €830,000-€1,000,000 (about $830k-$1.04M) ex-yard. Used 2021-2025 examples have listed roughly $725,000-$870,000; an early, well-equipped 2015 boat (Arctic Monkey) was offered at $549,000. Thin used supply (about ten boats a year built) keeps prices firm.

Garcia Exploration 45 — Arctic Monkey, Swiftsure Yachts

cleaned_facts.value: SAIL Magazine Best Boat / Boat of the Year 2015 and European Yacht of the Year (Luxury Cruiser category) — strong recognition for a purpose-built expedition aluminium centreboarder.

sources.title: SAIL Magazine — Garcia Exploration 45: Jimmy Cornell's Ideal Boat
sources.quote: The boat received SAIL Magazine's 2015 Best Boat award

removed.reason: Fabricated average not found in any source. PBO review says 80mm hull / 40mm deck; Garcia official says 74mm hull. The record's '77mm hull, 37mm deck' figures appear to be a blended/invented number. Corrected fact retained with actual source values.

No fetched source confirms the specific model designation 'Thermo 90ST' or '9 outlets.' Wings of Desire listing says only 'Webasto hydronic diesel heating system.' Overly precise model details without a confirmable source; retained fact with confirmed level of detail only.

Cited sources (garciayachts.com, sailingyahtzee.com) did not contain verifiable language about 'forward cabin window intentionally inclined to protect from tropical sun' or 'tinted/shaded viewport design.' Claim is too specific to retain without direct quote.

Garcia official site only confirms existence of VMC + Dorade boxes. No source verifies performance in high-ambient-temperature anchorages (Red Sea, SE Asia). Retained general ventilation fact under tropical.ventilation.
Confidence was 0.6 and sole source (bwsailing.com) only gave spec data, not a statement about absence of windvane. Absence-of-evidence claim with weak sourcing; cannot confirm either way.
Primary source (itboat.com) returned HTTP 403 and could not be verified. Model numbers are too specific to retain without direct verification. Retained as lower-confidence general claim.

removed.key: ergonomics.side_decks — 'MarineDeck cork/rubber' and 'welded aluminum toe rail with perforations'
removed.reason: PBO review did not mention cork/rubber decking or perforated toe rails in verifiable fetched content. Specific brand 'MarineDeck' and construction details unverified.
PBO review did not mention AIS beacons at all in fetched content. Precise count of 5 beacons has no verifiable source and appears fabricated.

removed.key: heavy_weather.cutter_staysail — Parasailor and specific sail inventory
removed.reason: Primary source (yachtworld.com 2025 listing) returned HTTP 403. Sail inventory details including 'Incidence solent,' Parasailor, Lazy-Bag/Lazy-Jack specifics cannot be verified. These are single-boat options, not fleet standards.

Swiftsure Yachts Arctic Monkey listing page is a sales listing, not a storm report. It contains no storm performance data. Storm facts retained solely from the Yahtzee blog which directly documents the passage.
Verified source (vdvmarine.com) gives $29–33/ft = $1,305–$1,485 for a 45-foot boat, not $1,800–$2,400. The record's cost range is inflated beyond what the cited source supports.
Cited source (YachtWorld 2023 Tobatoo listing) is a sales listing that would not contain repair cost data. No verifiable cost basis for this figure.
JPC Direct source is a UK service provider quoting £50 labor with unspecified parts costs. The $150–$1,000 USD range cannot be derived from this source and appears estimated without basis.

URL listed as 'boatsgeek.com/cost-for-standing-standing-rigging-replacement/' contains a doubled word ('standing-standing') indicating a likely fabricated or hallucinated URL. Not fetched, flagged as suspicious.
No fetched source explicitly confirms the centerboard retracts 'fully into bilges.' Plausible for design but unverified specific claim.

fabrication_flags: Insulation values '77mm hull / 37mm deck' appear to be a computed average of conflicting sources (80/40 from PBO, 74 from Garcia official) — no source states these numbers directly
MOB AIS beacon count of '5' has no traceable source in any fetched page and is likely generated
BoatsGeek URL 'boatsgeek.com/cost-for-standing-standing-rigging-replacement/' contains a duplicated word ('standing-standing') strongly suggesting a hallucinated URL
Swiftsure Yachts Arctic Monkey page cited as a 'storm report' is actually a boat sales listing with no storm performance data — the attribution is misleading
Marine survey cost range of $1,800–$2,400 is not supported by the cited vdvmarine.com source, which computes to $1,305–$1,485 for a 45-foot vessel
Webasto 'Thermo 90ST' model name and '9 outlets' are too precise to appear in any verified source — likely generated detail
itboat.com returned HTTP 403 for both cited URLs, making all facts exclusively sourced there (Dessalator D100/D60 watermaker models, B&G NAC3/Triton/AP44/WR10 autopilot model numbers) unverifiable
YachtWorld 2025 listing returned HTTP 403, making cutter rig sail inventory details (Incidence solent, Parasailor) unverifiable fleet-level claims
verify.verdict: Partially verified. Core structural and safety facts (CE Class A, STIX 37.5, ballast range, watertight bulkheads, inside helm, electrical system, gearbox failures, deck joint issue, storm performance) are confirmed by accessible sources. Seven facts were removed or demoted: the 77/37mm insulation values are fabricated averages; the AIS beacon count of 5 is invented; a BoatsGeek URL has a hallucinated doubled path segment; the Arctic Monkey source is a sales listing misrepresented as a storm report; the survey cost range overstates the cited source by 35–60%; Webasto model specifics are unverified over-precision; and all itboat.com-exclusive facts (Dessalator watermaker models, B&G autopilot model numbers) could not be checked due to HTTP 403. The overall record is directionally accurate for a blue-water expedition boat but contains meaningful invented precision, particularly in equipment model numbers and refit cost figures.

cold_score.rationale: This is one of the best cold-weather and high-latitude boats in this guide, and the score reflects that. It was designed from a blank sheet for the Northwest Passage, and it shows. Heating is a proper Webasto hydronic diesel system circulating hot water to outlets throughout the boat, so each cabin can be warmed independently. Insulation is genuinely expedition-grade: 80mm of polyethylene foam against the inside of the hull and 40mm under the deck, with double-glazed windows throughout to kill condensation and cold bridging. The standout is the inside steering and nav station with 270-degree visibility — you can stand watch in warmth and safety from below in foul weather, which testers have done in 40-knot conditions. Watertight bulkheads fore and aft and four-point-locking companionway doors seal the living space off from the sea. The only marks against it are minor: the mast is deck-stepped rather than keel-stepped, and the cockpit itself is open (the shelter is the inside helm, not a hard cockpit enclosure), with early boats criticised for restricted cockpit sightlines before later builds added a hard dodger. No data was found on heated or insulated water tankage, so that was left out of the assessment rather than counted against the boat.
cold_score.basis: Webasto hydronic heating, 80mm hull / 40mm deck insulation and double glazing, an inside helm with 270-degree visibility used on watch in 40-knot weather, and watertight fore-and-aft bulkheads — all confirmed by the PBO and SAIL reviews, the Arctic Monkey listing and Garcia's own material. Deck-stepped mast and open cockpit are the only cold-weather shortfalls.

tropic_score.rationale: Although it is best known as a polar boat, the Garcia was deliberately designed to work in the tropics too, and it scores well here. Ventilation is a real strength: obstructable Dorade boxes plus a forced mechanical ventilation system push air through the boat, and the same heating loop helps manage the cabin in any climate. The galley has two front-loading fridge drawers and a separate freezer for keeping provisions in heat. A watermaker is fitted on most cruising builds (a Dessalator or Echotec depending on the boat), which is what makes long tropical passages comfortable, though it is an option rather than fixed standard equipment. The main limit is sailing performance in the heat: this is a heavy displacement boat (about 14 tonnes) that is genuinely slow in light airs, so tropical passages can mean more motoring than on a lighter cruiser. The forward window is angled and the later boats gained a hard dodger, but we could not confirm a dedicated tropical-shade system from a reliable source, so that was set aside rather than scored either way.
tropic_score.basis: Dorade boxes plus forced mechanical ventilation, dual fridge drawers and a freezer, and a watermaker on most builds all support hot-climate cruising. The drag on the score is heavy displacement and poor light-air speed noted by reviewers. A specific tropical-shade design could not be verified and was excluded.

stability_score.rationale: Be honest about what is and isn't known here. The Garcia is certified to CE Category A (Open Ocean), meaning it is designed and built for voyages where the wind may exceed Force 8 and the seas exceed four metres, and that certification is the firm ground the score stands on. The owner's manual also quotes a stability index (STIX) of 37.5 at full load, which is a respectable number for a 45-footer. What is NOT published anywhere we could find is an Angle of Vanishing Stability (AVS) figure — the single most useful number for judging ultimate self-righting ability — so we will not invent or imply one. There is a genuine caveat: this is an unballasted-centreboard design with most of its weight as internal ballast rather than down in a deep keel bulb, and experienced sailors note that boats of this type can sit lower on the stability curve than a deep-keel boat. The aluminium hull with a composite-jointed coachroof and the centreboard configuration also add some uncertainty that a CE category alone does not capture. The score reflects strong, certified offshore capability tempered by the missing AVS figure and the inherent trade-off of the centreboard layout.
stability_score.basis: CE Category A (Open Ocean) is confirmed in the Garcia owner's manual, and the same manual quotes a full-load STIX of 37.5. No published AVS figure exists in any source we found. Centreboard boats can have lower ultimate stability than deep-keel designs, which is reflected in the confidence.

storm_verdict.text: Few boats in this guide have a better-documented heavy-weather and expedition pedigree. The boat's designer-owner, Jimmy Cornell, sailed his own Exploration 45 (Aventura IV) across the Drake Passage to Antarctica twice, through the Northwest Passage and around Alaska — including a hard grounding the centreboard absorbed by swinging up, and a collision with an iceberg's underwater ledge — and on one engine-troubled Greenland-to-England passage met all the boat's electrical needs from solar, wind and a towed hydrogenerator alone. Separately, a 530-mile Atlantic delivery in sustained 30-40 knot winds saw the boat surf steadily at 10-13 knots, touch 15.5 knots, and stay quiet and dry below thanks to its watertight companionway doors, with the crew standing watch in comfort from the inside helm. The aluminium hull, watertight bow and stern bulkheads, twin skeg-protected rudders and CE Category A certification are exactly what you want for serious high-latitude work, and they are backed by real ocean miles rather than brochure claims. The honest caveat is that we did not find a detailed first-hand account of survival conditions above Force 9 (heaving-to, lying to a drogue, or true storm behaviour), so while everything points to a very capable storm boat, its outright survival-storm behaviour is inferred from design and track record rather than fully documented.

by_item.note: Standing rigging is typically replaced every 15-20 years, so a 2015 boat is approaching but not yet at that point if it has been well maintained. The cutter rig adds an inner forestay and running backstays over a simple sloop, which is why the figure sits at the upper end of the range. Inspect before purchase and replace immediately if any wire shows broken (fishhook) strands or cracked swage terminals.
Running rigging usually needs replacing every 8-10 years, so an 11-year-old boat is past due. The cutter rig adds extra sheets and a staysail halyard. Budget for a full set at purchase.

Sails are generally good for 8-12 years, so an 11-year-old boat probably needs at least new headsails. This covers a full wardrobe (main, furling genoa, staysail) for a 45ft expedition boat. Laminate sails degrade faster than cruising Dacron under UV, so check what is fitted.

by_item.system: Volvo D2-75 Gearbox Rebuild (confirmed production defect)
by_item.note: This is not routine wear — it is a confirmed defect on the original Volvo D2-75 mechanical gearbox, with clutch failures reported roughly every 200 hours. Assume a rebuild is needed unless the seller can show the gearbox was replaced or the defect was resolved. A full engine repower is not yet due at this age (engines typically last 25-30 years), so only the gearbox is flagged here.

Marine electronics typically last 10-15 years, so an 11-year-old boat is right at the edge. Chartplotters, VHF, AIS, autopilot control heads and instruments all need evaluation. A full expedition fit-out (radar, plus offshore comms such as Iridium or Starlink) pushes toward the top of the range. The exact B&G autopilot package on a given boat should be checked on survey.

AGM battery banks last about 6-10 years, so the Victron Supercycle AGMs on an 11-year-old boat are almost certainly worn out. A like-for-like AGM replacement of a 1000Ah bank runs about $8k-15k; converting to lithium with a battery management system runs $12k-20k. The low end is an AGM swap; the high end is the lithium upgrade.

An expedition boat should carry two anchors, and the chain needs a stretch and gauge check (galvanized or G4). Windlass service is included here. The low end assumes the chain is reusable if within spec; the high end covers full chain replacement.

Seacocks are typically a 25-year item, so not due for wholesale replacement on an 11-year-old boat. Garcia uses welded aluminium through-hulls with valves above the waterline, which sidesteps the usual bronze-in-aluminium galvanic worry, but the fittings still need inspection. The low end is inspection and service; the high end covers replacing any questionable valves.
The stern platform has a dedicated liferaft locker, so stowage is sorted. Budget covers an offshore-rated liferaft plus EPIRB, flares, jacklines, tethers and MOB gear for a passage-ready fit.

by_item.system: Canvas (dodger, bimini, sail covers, winch covers)
by_item.note: Canvas typically lasts 10-15 years, so an 11-year-old boat is at the edge. The Garcia's hard dodger top is durable, but the soft bimini extensions and sail covers will be UV-degraded by now. This is a mid-range estimate.

A watermaker is fitted on most cruising builds but is not universal, so check the specific boat. If a serviceable unit is present, membrane replacement and service runs about $3k-5k; if absent, a new installation runs about $6k-10k. The range covers both cases.

Garcia-specific confirmed defect: dissimilar material joint between aluminum hull and GRP cabin top. YBW forum confirms chronic leaks from differential thermal expansion. Budget for re-bedding, sealant treatment, and epoxy fairing. This is a provisioning-for-passage item, not a service-interval item.

PBO review documents leaking hatches and instrument seals on early Version 1 hulls. If buying a pre-2018 build, assume this is needed. Pressure testing recommended as part of pre-purchase survey. Low end is sealant re-bed only; high end includes hatch replacement.

Garcia-specific: aluminum hull requires correct sacrificial anode specification and verified electrical isolation from bronze/stainless fittings. Galvanic corrosion is an existential threat to aluminum hulls. Inspection and anode replacement at every haulout; included here as passage-readiness baseline cost.

Garcia-specific: strip the old antifouling, inspect the bare aluminium for pitting, then apply an aluminium-compatible barrier and copper-free antifouling. Copper-based paint in contact with the hull is actively harmful, so this is not the same job as a routine fiberglass repaint and should be quoted by a yard that knows aluminium. Low end is a straightforward recoat; high end is a full strip-to-metal and barrier scheme.

Garcia-specific: fit or verify a galvanic isolator or isolation transformer so the hull is protected from marina stray current, which is the main external corrosion threat to an aluminium boat. Cheap relative to the consequences. Low end is a galvanic isolator; high end is a full isolation transformer installation.

refit_projection.assumptions: These figures model an early (around 2015) boat, roughly eleven years old, being brought up to passage-ready standard. Replacement timings are the usual marine service intervals for each system. On top of the standard wear items, we have added three Garcia-specific lines — the Volvo D2-75 gearbox rebuild, the aluminium-to-fiberglass deck-joint re-seal, and aluminium-specific anode and bottom work — because these are confirmed issues for this boat rather than generic wear. We have also added two aluminium-only items most buyers overlook: a bottom strip and repaint with an aluminium-safe, copper-free paint system, and a shore-power galvanic isolator, because using the wrong paint or leaving the hull exposed to marina stray current is a real corrosion risk on a metal boat. A full engine repower is not included because the engine itself is not yet old enough to need one, though the gearbox is treated as a near-certain line item given the documented failure pattern. Electronics and batteries are costed as full replacements rather than partial upgrades, in keeping with preparing the boat for offshore use. All costs are estimates; real quotes vary widely with the yard, the country, labour rates and the individual boat's condition, so treat these as a planning range and confirm against a survey.

provisioning_specific.item: Volvo D2-75 gearbox rebuild parts kit and spare clutch assembly
provisioning_specific.why: Confirmed production defect with clutch failures recurring every ~200 hours. Offshore passage without a rebuild kit and documented clutch spares is imprudent given this specific failure mode. Carry both rebuild kit and a complete spare gearbox if budget allows.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare aluminum welding rod stock and contact details for a certified aluminum TIG welder at intended destination ports
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum hull repairs require specialized fabrication unavailable at most boatyards. The YBW forum documents dubious welding quality in early bow sections. Pre-identify aluminum-capable yards in passage plan; carry 3003 and 5086 aluminum rod stock.

provisioning_specific.item: Proprietary Garcia deck-joint sealant and butyl tape stock for ongoing re-bedding
provisioning_specific.why: The aluminum-to-GRP deck joint is a chronic leak source confirmed by multiple owners and the YBW forum. Carry sufficient sealant (Sikaflex 291 or equivalent) and butyl tape to re-bed any hatch or joint that weeps on passage — this is not a once-and-done repair.

provisioning_specific.item: Webasto hydronic heater service kit (glow plug, gasket set, burner chamber components)
provisioning_specific.why: Webasto hydronic systems are reliable but glow plug and gasket wear are the primary service items. Heating is one of this boat's biggest cold-climate strengths, and in high latitudes losing it is a safety problem, not just a comfort one. Parts are not universally stocked outside Europe, so carry a service kit.

provisioning_specific.item: Victron MPPT controller firmware update and spare BMV battery monitor
provisioning_specific.why: The confirmed 1,125W solar array and 1,000Ah bank are a core electrical autonomy asset. A failed MPPT controller in a remote anchorage can strand battery charging. Carry one spare Victron MPPT and a BMV-712 monitor as minimum; Victron firmware should be current before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Centerboard cable, thimbles, and winch service kit
provisioning_specific.why: The centerboard is a unique Garcia system with no documented standard replacement interval. Cable stretch and winch wear are expected on older boats. The board provides shoal-draft access that is a primary design advantage; carry a full cable replacement kit so the board can be serviced on passage.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare zinc anodes (hull, shaft, rudder) sized for aluminum hull specification
provisioning_specific.why: Galvanic corrosion is an existential threat to aluminum hulls. Anode depletion rate varies dramatically by marina electrical environment. Carry three full sets of replacement anodes and test bonding continuity at every haulout. Using incorrect anode alloy (zinc vs magnesium vs aluminum) accelerates hull corrosion.

provisioning_specific.item: Echotec or Dessalator watermaker membrane and pre-filter cartridge stock (12-month supply)
provisioning_specific.why: The confirmed Echotec 12V 260DL unit (Wings of Desire) and Dessalator options cited require membranes and pre-filter cartridges that are not available in all cruising grounds. Membrane life is 3-5 years or 2,000 hours. Carry one spare membrane and 12 months of pre-filters.

provisioning_specific.item: Watt and Sea Cruising 600 hydrogenator spare impeller and bearing kit
provisioning_specific.why: The Watt and Sea Cruising 600 is confirmed on at least one well-equipped build and is a primary underway charging source at passage speeds. Impeller wear and bearing failure are the primary service items; parts require ordering from France and have multi-week lead times.

provisioning_specific.item: Double-glazed portlight gasket stock and hatch seal tape
provisioning_specific.why: Double-glazed windows are confirmed insulation assets (cold score contribution). PBO review and YBW forum document early-hull hatch and portlight leaks. Gasket degradation is accelerated by UV exposure and salt cycles. Carry full-perimeter gasket stock for all opening ports and hatches.

field_reports.title: Iceberg ledge strike spares hull
field_reports.account: Jimmy Cornell recounts striking an underwater rock ledge in the Northwest Passage hard enough to be knocked off his feet aboard his Garcia Exploration 45 Aventura IV. The rudder's sacrificial 'crumple zone' at the top of the blade absorbed the impact and prevented any hull damage; only superficial rudder repairs were needed later back in Cherbourg. He cites this as validation of the boat's protected twin-rudder design for ice and grounding-prone waters.

field_reports.title: Gooseneck fails in Greenland gybe
field_reports.account: On a southbound passage from Greenland sailing with his daughter Doina, Cornell suffered an involuntary gybe triggered by a large breaking wave that damaged the boom gooseneck casting on Aventura IV. He lashed the boom to the mast winches and continued 1,100 miles to St John's, Newfoundland in seven days, where a workshop machined a solid-aluminium replacement. He credits a boom brake with preventing far worse damage in the gybe.
field_reports.conditions: large breaking wave caused an involuntary gybe

source.title: PBO — Jimmy Cornell: How I've learned to fix my own problems at sea

field_reports.title: Centreboarder stable in 60-knot winds
field_reports.account: Drawing on having sailed twice to Antarctica on centreboard boats, Cornell argues the type is as stable as keeled yachts in extreme conditions, citing weathering 60-knot winds. On Aventura IV he describes roughly 3.5 tonnes of internal lead ballast against a ~9-tonne displacement, and notes retracting the board reduces wetted surface to add 0.3–0.5 knots of speed off the wind. He concedes hard upwind sailing is the centreboarder's weakest point.

field_reports.title: Upwind is the weak point
field_reports.account: Cornell candidly states that sailing hard on the wind is the weakest point of a centreboard boat, and recounts reluctantly abandoning the yankee-and-staysail cutter rig he used on Aventura III for a Solent configuration on the Exploration 45. He reports the 7/8 fractional rig will make roughly 35 degrees to the apparent wind when well trimmed, and that the boat touched 9.5 knots under full staysail with two reefs in. The honesty about windward limits is a recurring owner theme.

field_reports.title: Surfs to 15.5kn in Atlantic gale
field_reports.region: Maine to Maryland, US East Coast
field_reports.account: The cruising crew of Yahtzee delivered the Exploration 45 Arctic Monkey 530 miles from Portland, Maine to Annapolis in demanding conditions, meeting 30-plus knots of wind and large swells. The boat surfed at 10–13 knots with a peak of 15.5 knots, averaging over 7 knots for the passage, and they called her 'a smooth-handling rocketship' off the wind with the twin rudders holding course reliably. They singled out watertight doors as 'priceless' when it blew 40 knots and the forward nav station for night watches.
field_reports.conditions: 30+ knots wind, large swells, gusts to 40 knots

source.title: Three Sheets / Sailing Yahtzee — Garcia Exploration 45 revisited after a real boat test

field_reports.title: Board-up running 'straight and true'
field_reports.account: Ex-Vendee Globe racer Pete Goss and his wife Tracey crossed from Guernsey to Antigua via Lanzarote aboard their Exploration 45 Pearl of Penzance, leaving in winter and arriving on Christmas Day. They met 30 knots near Ushant and a week of strong winds and big seas in the Atlantic, then surfed at 17 knots approaching Antigua. Goss reports that with the centreboard raised the boat 'just runs with the wave, straight and true,' and that the B&G autopilot needed minimal correction while surfing.
field_reports.conditions: 30 knots near Ushant; ~1 week of strong winds and big seas; surfs to 17 knots

source.title: Yachting World — Pete Goss transatlantic: Vendee Globe racer on life in the slow lane

field_reports.title: 17,000 miles of liveaboard lessons
field_reports.account: After about 17,000 miles cruising from the UK to the Caribbean and the US east coast on Pearl of Penzance, Pete Goss reflects that roughly 20% of cruising time is actually spent sailing and the rest at anchor. He praises the centreboard for reaching shallow anchorages closed to keelboats, the pilothouse for a light airy interior with good weather visibility, and the full-bodied hull for form stability in rolly anchorages. He flags spares-and-customs frustration with custom parts and runs four independent charging sources (solar, wind, hydro, engine).

source.title: Yachting World — Pete Goss's lessons learned from 2.5 years cruising the world

field_reports.title: Three Garcias clear the Northwest Passage
field_reports.account: In summer 2024 three owner-operated Exploration 45s — Voyager (Switzerland), Night Owl (Britain) and Hauru (Poland) — transited the Northwest Passage together, covering about 3,600 miles from Greenland's west coast to the Bering Strait in roughly six weeks. They waited off Greenland for ice to clear before entering Lancaster Sound, then worked south via Prince Regent Inlet to the notorious Bellot Strait. The shared transit cemented the model's high-latitude reputation.
field_reports.conditions: drifting pack ice, fog and frequent icebergs

source.title: Garcia Yachts — Our three Garcia Exploration 45s made it through the Northwest Passage

field_reports.title: Bellot Strait rescue, pristine hull after ice
field_reports.account: Aboard Voyager, Adriano Vigano and Marisa Lankester made multiple attempts at the 18-mile Bellot Strait, initially blocked by closing ice, and rescued the crew of a French yacht that was 'pushed out of the water like a cork from a bottle' between converging floes. Vigano reports the boat 'copes incredibly well with heavy weather,' citing earlier North Sea testing, and that Voyager's bare aluminium hull came through twelve weeks in ice nearly pristine. The elevated saloon let the crew keep watch and work from a protected interior.
field_reports.conditions: shifting pack ice, fog, frequent icebergs; severe weather tested earlier on North Sea

source.title: YACHT — Northwest Passage: dangerous route through the ice with three Garcia yachts

field_reports.title: Comfortable in a confused sea
field_reports.account: Yachting Monthly's sea-trial found the rounded aluminium hull suffers to windward from the lack of low-down ballast typical of centreboarders, but on a windy test in a confused sea the boat was 'very comfortable' and the Jefa helm was responsive and lighter than her 14-tonne bulk suggests. The reviewer notes that with the wind aft of the beam you can start lifting the board, which lightens the helm and improves performance. The deep skeg adds directional stability at the cost of marina manoeuvrability.
field_reports.conditions: windy test sail in a confused sea

field_reports.title: 6.5kn upwind, 8kn reaching on Chesapeake
field_reports.account: A Blue Water Sailing / Cruising Compass sea trial of the Exploration 45 in about 20 knots and building chop on Chesapeake Bay logged 6.5 knots close-hauled at 35–40 degrees apparent and 7.5 knots sustained (8-knot peaks) on a beam reach, with twin-rudder steering described as effortless. The reviewer praised deck ergonomics, granny bars at the mast, and quiet sightlines from the forward nav station. A delivery-crew member called her as comfortable underway as anything they had sailed.

source.title: Cruising Compass / Blue Water Sailing — Garcia Exploration 45

field_reports.title: Greenland-to-England on renewables alone
field_reports.account: Cornell reports that on a Greenland-to-England passage where Aventura IV had engine problems, the boat's wind, hydro and solar generation 'provided all our energy needs perfectly,' validating his refusal to fit a diesel generator. He also describes routinely drying the boat out on a beach for maintenance and wildlife watching thanks to the fully retracting centreboard. Insulation above the waterline and an insulated floor are cited as meaningful advantages for managing condensation when living aboard in cold, damp high latitudes.

field_reports.title: Decade of poles aboard a Garcia 52
field_reports.account: The Italian-owned Exploration 52 Lifexplorer (delivered April 2017) ran a ten-year program from 80°N in the Arctic Ocean off Svalbard down to Patagonia and beyond 62°S at the Antarctic Peninsula. A 350-mile Barents Sea leg took 42 hours in 25–35 knots with surfs to 12 knots over a current-roughened sea, averaging over 8 knots, and the aluminium boat logged 5,000-plus miles in the northern phase with minimal issues. The owners cite excellent range and provisioning capacity for remote, self-sufficient sailing.
field_reports.conditions: 25–35 knots, rough current-affected sea, surfs to 12 knots (Barents Sea)

source.title: Garcia Yachts — Lifexplorer: 10 years of sailing in extreme latitudes aboard a Garcia Exploration 52

field_reports.title: Atlantic crossing on the bigger sister
field_reports.account: Yachting World tester Francois Tregouet crossed the Atlantic west-to-east on the Exploration 60 Albius IV, Le Marin to Horta in 14 days 20 hours with a crew of four. Conditions were light for much of the way — 12–20 knots early, a calm stretch under engine, then a Code 0 spell — with boatspeed staying a remarkably constant 7–9 knots when trimmed, and the centreboard credited for steady motion despite heel. The cabin beneath the saloon was rated one of the most comfortable berths in rough seas, illustrating the line's seakindly liveaboard character.
field_reports.conditions: 12–20 knots early then light/calm; speeds held 7–9 knots

source.title: Yachting World — Garcia Exploration 60 review: we crossed the Atlantic to test it
